sound_type: musical multi-note whistle -- series of 3-5 ascending or descending tonal notes with musical phrasing, the most melodically complex mammalian vocalization in the engine, E=4, endemic to Olympic Peninsula
ecosystem: alpine and subalpine meadows of the Olympic Mountains, 1,500-2,000 meters. ENDEMIC -- found only in the Olympics. Isolated by the Puget Sound lowlands. A sky island species.
season: active spring through fall (May-September); HIBERNATES 6-8 months (September-May). Communal family hibernation.

species_pair.pair_type: Marmot TRIO -- THIRD consecutive Sciuridae (75-76-77). Unprecedented: no family has appeared three times consecutively. All three PNW marmots: M. flaviventris (75), M. caligata (76), M. olympus (77). Three acoustic signatures: chirp, penetrating whistle, multi-note musical whistle. Three geographic ranges: widespread, widespread, ENDEMIC.

coordinates.description: Reference for Olympic alpine habitat, Hurricane Ridge area, Olympic National Park. The Olympic Marmot is found ONLY in the alpine and subalpine zones of the Olympic Mountains. Endemic to the Olympic Peninsula.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRD CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE (75-76-77). The Sciuridae trio. Marmot trio. ENDEMIC to Olympic Peninsula. Sixteenth mammalian entry. Fourteenth mammalian species. Fourth Rodentia. Sciuridae's third entry. All three PNW marmots complete.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Marmota from French marmotte, from Latin murem montis (mouse of the mountain). olympus from the Olympic Mountains. Named by Merriam (1898). Marmota olympus = 'the mountain mouse of Olympus.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Marmota -- approximately 14 species worldwide. THREE in the PNW, ALL NOW IN THE ENGINE: M. flaviventris (75), M. caligata (76), M. olympus (77). PNW marmot genus COMPLETE.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- fourth Rodentia in the engine after Beaver (61-62), Yellow-bellied Marmot (75), and Hoary Marmot (76).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- sixteenth mammalian entry. Fourteenth mammalian species. Four orders. Nine families. Two infraclasses.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's THIRD entry at degree 77. Third consecutive (75-76-77). Unprecedented trio. Genus Marmota complete for PNW.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ALARM/COMMUNICATION WHISTLE with musical phrasing. Series of 3-5 ascending or descending tonal steps. More melodically complex than other marmot calls.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 1.0-2.5 seconds for a full phrase. Individual notes 0.2-0.4 seconds each.
song_metrics.note_structure: Multi-note series: 3-5 distinct tonal steps, ascending or descending. Clear pitch separation between notes. Musical phrasing.
song_metrics.note_types: Alarm whistle (multi-note, primary), territorial call, social greeting. The multi-note structure may encode predator type or urgency.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Multi-note phrase: 3-5 notes in sequence. More complex than single-note marmot calls. Closer to birdsong phrasing than typical mammalian alarm.
song_metrics.timbre: Musical, tonal, clear. Each note is a clean whistle with pitch variation between notes. The overall impression is melodic -- the 'singing marmot.'
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderate. Audible at 100-300 meters. Lower than Hoary Marmot's E=5 but more melodically complex. The trade: volume for musical complexity.
song_metrics.tempo: Multi-note phrases given in series when alarmed. Inter-phrase interval: 2-5 seconds. More sustained calling than Hoary Marmot's short bursts.
song_metrics.context: ALARM and SOCIAL COMMUNICATION. Multi-note structure used for colony warning, territorial signaling, and social greeting. The musical complexity may serve communicative functions within the highly social family colonies.
song_metrics.regional_variation: No geographic variation -- species is endemic to one mountain range. The Olympic Peninsula IS the entire range.
song_metrics.key_property: MUSICAL MULTI-NOTE WHISTLE -- the 78th acoustic element. Series of 3-5 tonal notes. Musical phrasing. E=4. The most melodically complex mammalian call in the engine. Heard only in the Olympic Mountains.

physical_description.overall: Stocky ground squirrel. 560-680 mm total length. 3.5-6 kg at peak autumn mass. Intermediate between Yellow-bellied and Hoary Marmots. Brown to tawny-brown pelage. White muzzle patches. ENDEMIC to Olympic Peninsula.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 77: (1) BROWN PELAGE -- warm brown, distinct from silver-gray Hoary and yellowish-brown Yellow-bellied. (2) INTERMEDIATE SIZE -- 3.5-6 kg. (3) OLYMPIC ALPINE HABITAT -- found ONLY in Olympic Mountains. (4) WHITE MUZZLE PATCHES. (5) MULTI-NOTE WHISTLE -- musical phrasing, 3-5 notes.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Brown to tawny-brown. HEAD: Dark brown crown, white muzzle patches. EXTREMITIES: Darker brown but less distinct 'boots' than Hoary. BELLY: Lighter tan to buff. TAIL: Brown, 170-240 mm.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition with ever-growing incisors. Herbivorous. Alpine wildflowers, grasses, sedges.
physical_description.field_id: In Olympic Mountain alpine meadows ONLY: a medium-large brown marmot with white muzzle patches. Distinguished from Hoary Marmot by brown (not silver-gray) color and lower elevation. If you're in the Olympics above treeline and see a brown marmot, it's this species. Multi-note musical whistle is diagnostic.

endemic_note.description: ENDEMIC to the Olympic Peninsula. Found NOWHERE else on Earth. Total range approximately 500 km2 of suitable alpine habitat in the Olympic Mountains. Isolated by Puget Sound lowlands. Sky island species. The most geographically restricted species in the engine.
endemic_note.conservation: Population declined. Estimated fewer than 2,000 individuals. Primary threat: coyote predation (coyotes expanded into Olympic alpine). Climate change shrinks alpine habitat. Olympic National Park provides habitat protection but species has no buffer population.
endemic_note.significance: The engine's first endemic species. Geographic restriction is extreme -- single mountain range. The Olympic Marmot demonstrates the vulnerability of sky island species to environmental change.

nodes.title: Endemic to Olympic Peninsula -- Found Nowhere Else
nodes.description: The Olympic Marmot exists ONLY in the Olympic Mountains. Most geographically restricted species in the engine. Sky island species. Approximately 500 km2 of total range. Isolated by Puget Sound lowlands.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Trio (75-76-77) -- Marmot Trio
nodes.description: Three consecutive Sciuridae. Unprecedented -- no family has appeared three times consecutively. All three PNW marmots: Yellow-bellied (75), Hoary (76), Olympic (77). Chirp, whistle, multi-note whistle. Three acoustic signatures, three geographic ranges.

nodes.title: Musical Multi-Note Whistle -- 78th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Series of 3-5 ascending or descending tonal notes. Musical phrasing. E=4. The most melodically complex mammalian vocalization in the engine. 1,500-6,000 Hz. Duration 1.0-2.5 seconds.

nodes.title: Fourth Consecutive Alpine (74-75-76-77)
nodes.description: Pika (74), Yellow-bellied (75), Hoary (76), Olympic (77). Four consecutive alpine species. The longest habitat streak in the engine.

nodes.title: Olympic Peninsula Thread (1, 4, 77)
nodes.description: Pacific Wren (1, Olympic rainforest), Spotted Owl (4, Olympic old-growth), Olympic Marmot (77, Olympic alpine). Three altitudinal zones of a single peninsula. Spans nearly the entire engine.

nodes.title: Pika Contrast -- Same Habitat, Different Strategies
nodes.description: Olympic Marmot: fat-storing, hibernates 6-8 months. Pika: hay-storing, active year-round. Same alpine zone. Different solutions. The Olympic Marmot adds communal family hibernation.

nodes.title: The Mree Parallel
nodes.description: Both create layered melody. Both geographically specific (Seattle bedroom pop / Olympic alpine). Both quiet relative to peers (E=2 / E=4 vs. Hoary E=5). Three dimensions of parallel.

nodes.title: Hoary Marmot Connection (SPS-076)
nodes.description: Marmot trio. E=5 vs. E=4. Single whistle vs. multi-note. Widespread vs. endemic. Larger vs. intermediate. The louder relative versus the rarer endemic.

nodes.title: Yellow-bellied Marmot Connection (SPS-075)
nodes.description: Marmot trio. Chirp vs. multi-note whistle. Most widespread vs. most restricted. Three PNW marmots complete.

cross_references.relationship: MARMOT TRIO (75-76-77). E=5 vs. E=4. Single penetrating whistle vs. multi-note musical whistle. Widespread (PNW to Alaska) vs. ENDEMIC (Olympic only). Larger (5-7 kg) vs. intermediate (3.5-6 kg). Silver-gray vs. brown.
MARMOT TRIO. Three PNW marmots complete. Sharp chirp vs. multi-note whistle. Most widespread vs. most restricted. Eastern Cascades/Great Basin vs. Olympic Peninsula.
FOURTH CONSECUTIVE ALPINE (74-77). Same high-elevation habitat. Different strategies: Pika stores hay, no hibernation. Olympic Marmot stores fat, hibernates 6-8 months communally.
OLYMPIC PENINSULA CONNECTION. Both Olympic Peninsula specialists. Owl in old-growth forest (low-mid elevation). Marmot in alpine meadow (high). Same peninsula, different altitudes. 73 degrees apart.
OLYMPIC PENINSULA CONNECTION. Wren in Olympic rainforest (low). Marmot in Olympic alpine (high). Degree 1 and degree 77. Same peninsula, maximum altitudinal separation.
Sixteenth mammalian entry. Fourteenth species. Fourth Rodentia. Sciuridae trio (75-76-77). Endemic. All three PNW marmots complete.
Olympic alpine meadow habitat. ENDEMIC to Olympic Peninsula. Sky island ecology. Communal family hibernation. Climate-sensitive. Coyote predation driving decline.
